data.title: Centessa rated Outperform at Oppenheimer on ORX750 potential
data.description:
Centessa Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:CNTA) has been rated as Outperform at Oppenheimer, reflecting strong conviction in the company's lead asset, ORX750, an orexin-2 receptor agonist.

This upgrade comes ahead of the anticipated phase 2a CRYSTAL-1 trial data expected by Q4 2025, which will provide insights into ORX750's efficacy for idiopathic hypersomnia and narcolepsy types 1 and 2.

Oppenheimer also underscores the large market opportunity for ORX750, driven by current polypharmacy dynamics in sleep disorder treatments and a commercial landscape shaped by competitors like TAK-861, which may help raise market awareness benefiting ORX750’s uptake as a fast follower.

The research firm has set a price target of $40 for the company.

data.title: Ten years after Buffett's big deal, Kraft Heinz eyes a breakup
data.description:
The Kraft Heinz Company (NASDAQ:KHC) is reportedly close to finalizing a breakup of one of the country's largest food and beverage company into two entities, according to The Wall Street Journal, with an announcement likely to come next week.

Sources cited by the newspaper claim the food giant will split off its grocery business – which includes products like Kraft’s Macaroni & Cheese, Velveeta, Jell-O, and Kool-Aid – into an entity that would have a value of around $20M, with the remaining faster-growing segment that includes ketchup and sauces forming a separate and smaller entity. The transaction would essentially undo the 2015 merger between Kraft Foods Group and H.J. Heinz Company which created the third-largest food and beverage company in the U.S.

The combination was orchestrated in 2015 by Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway and 3G Capital under the terms which gave Kraft shareholders a 49% stake in the combined company and Heinz shareholders a 51% stake. Kraft shareholders received stock in the combined company and a special cash dividend of $16.50 per share, fully funded by an equity contribution by Heinz owners Berkshire Hathaway and 3G Capital. At the time of the merger, the two companies had combined revenue of ~$28B, an amount that would subsequently dwindle to $6.35B. 

“I am delighted to play a part in bringing these two winning companies and their iconic brands together. This is my kind of transaction, uniting two world-class organizations and delivering shareholder value. I’m excited by the opportunities for what this new combined organization will achieve,” Warren Buffett said at the time of the merger.

In an effort to boost profits, the newly-formed Kraft Heinz Company (NASDAQ:KHC) immediately went to work cutting costs and going on a buying spree which included a failed attempt to acquire Unilever (UL) for $143B in 2017. 

Unfortunately, while the company was busy cost-cutting and looking for new toys, it failed to notice the shift in consumer tastes to healthier food options. With sales down amid a pivot away from processed cheese and hot dogs, the company finally conceded that its Kraft and Oscar Mayer brands were worth much less than it was valued at and took a $15B impairment charge. Additionally, then-CEO Bernardo Hess admitted that zero-based budgeting (in which every expense must be justified from a zero-base for each new budgeting period) had failed.

“We were overly optimistic on delivering savings that did not materialize,” Hess said in 2019.

Amid all the hand-wringing, the stock price continued to drop. After peaking in 2017, the company’s shares lost 61% of their value in the 10 years since the merger versus a 237% gain in the S&P 500. This led Berkshire Hathaway to take a second write-down on its 27.4% stake in Kraft Heinz (NASDAQ:KHC), this time for $3.8B after a $3B write-down in 2019.

Plagued by increased manufacturing costs, Kraft Heinz’s profitability was also dwindling and by 2025 the company fell into the red.  

With few options left, the company is now considering splitting itself into two, a strategy that is unlikely to resuscitate the struggling food and beverage business.

“I do not see how the separation will raise any value,” says Seeking Alpha analyst Alan Galecki. “My concern is that we’ll end up with two sick entities.”

“The proposed spin-off will require strong P&L gains between the two distinct entities, and empirical evidence suggests the KHC spinoff is not likely to produce new value for shareholders,” Seeking Alpha analysts TQP Research adds.

And Wall Street likely agrees. Since the divestiture talk first started circulating last month, Kraft Heinz (KHC) shares have only gained 3% in value.

data.title: Southwest Gas raised to Buy at BofA as Centuri sale unlocks growth potential
data.description:
Southwest Gas (NYSE:SWX) +2% in Friday's trading as Bank of America upgraded to Buy from Neutral with an $84 price target, raised from $76, at Bank of America, citing the company's deal to sell its interest in Centuri Holdings that "will result in higher earnings and rate base growth, and an improved valuation."

Southwest Gas (NYSE:SWX) sold down ~$350M of its Centuri interest on August 7, taking its pro-forma ownership to 31%, and said it will account for its remaining interest using equity method accounting, prompting BofA analyst Ross Fowler to "believe there is now a clear path to completely exit the CTRI interest, thus removing a key investor overhang that has pressured valuation."

BofA's Fowler said Southwest Gas (SWX) trades at a ~1% discount to gas LDC peers compared to an average 3% premium historically, a discount that is unwarranted given the de-risked balance sheet following the Centuri sell-down, above average earnings per share compound annual growth rate of ~12%, a pending Q3 update on the Great Basin expansion project, and a constructive regulatory outlook.

data.title: Google to face 'modest' fine in EU ad-tech probe: Reuters
data.description:
A four-year European probe into Google's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (GOOGL) advertising technology practices is reportedly set to end up in a "modest" fine, particularly compared to past penalties.

The coming weeks will bring a light penalty from the European Commission (the European Union's competition watchdog), Reuters reported, citing three sources with direct knowledge.

And that signals a departure under EC chief Teresa Ribera from the previous regime led by Margrethe Vestager -- toward more practice-based remedies rather than fines. Ribera is not set to order Google to sell part of the ad-tech business, Reuters noted.

The fine isn't likely to come in around the scale of the €4.3B fine that Google faced in Europe tied to an antitrust probe of its Android mobile operating system, according to the report. Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (GOOGL) also faced smaller European fines over the past few years: of €2.42B linked to its shopping services, and €1.49B over Google's leveraging of its AdSense platform.

After a generally flat day, stock in Google parent Alphabet rose following the report: (GOOG) +0.9%, (GOOGL) +0.8%.

data.title: Billion Group files for IPO of 1.6M shares
data.description:
Billion Group Holdings has filed a registration statement with the Securities and Exchange Commission for an IPO of 1.6M shares.

The preliminary prospectus is not an offer to sell the securities.

The IPO price is currently estimated in the range of $4.00 to $6.00 per share.

The Hong Kong-based company expects to submit an application to have its ordinary shares listed on Nasdaq under the ticker symbol BGHL.

data.title: AI’s true impact on jobs will unfold over the next decade, not next year – MetLife Investment’s Drew Matus
data.description:
The real impact of artificial intelligence (NYSEARCA:AIEQ), (ARTY) on jobs and the economy will likely unfold over the next decade rather than in the immediate future, according to Drew Matus, MetLife Investment Management chief market strategist, who sees opportunities in technology (XLK), (IYW) and financial (XLF), (VFH) sectors despite current market volatility.

Matus shared this perspective in an interview with CNBC, suggesting that investment rather than consumption will drive future economic growth.

“If we go back 30 years when computers came onto the scene, basically entry level workers were being replaced by computers. And now you’re going to have another wave of that,” he said, adding that AI will gradually transform the job market in ways similar to previous technological revolutions, ultimately creating more opportunities than it eliminates.

He said that AI will reduce repetitive jobs while creating new positions that leverage human creativity. “We’re going to have less jobs that are day-to-day things; the jobs where people feel like they’re stuck,” he said. “Hopefully, people will actually create new jobs that encourage people’s creative sides, and they’re able to use AI to kind of create new things.”

Looking at investment opportunities, Matus highlighted technology (XLK) and financial (XLF) sectors as areas positioned for long-term growth. However, he cautioned about potential disruption, pointing out that when comparing top American companies from 30 years ago to today, “there’s really no overlap,” suggesting AI-native companies could displace established firms that fail to adapt.

In the long run, Matus remains optimistic that AI integration will lead to “a higher productivity regime” that initially creates labor market disruptions but ultimately generates “more jobs because it creates higher growth.”

The key challenge for existing businesses will be recognizing and responding to AI not just as an opportunity but as “a potential threat to their structure.”

data.title: Elon Musk's xAI sues former employee over allegedly taking info to OpenAI: report
data.description:
• Elon Musk's generative artificial intelligence company xAI has sued a former employee, alleging that they took proprietary information to competitor OpenAI, Reuters reported.
  • According to the complaint, which was filed in California, xAI alleged that Xuechen Li took “cutting-edge AI technologies with features superior to those offered by ChatGPT” when he left the Musk-led xAI, the news outlet added. He started at OpenAI this month. OpenAI is not listed as a defendant in the filing, Reuters added.
  • The maker of the Grok chatbot, xAI is asking for monetary damages and disallowing Li's move to OpenAI.
  • None of Li, xAI or OpenAI responded to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
  • The dispute over the former xAI employee is the latest battle between Musk and OpenAI. Musk, who co-founded OpenAI in 2015, along with CEO Sam Altman and Greg Brockman, left the company in 2018. 
  • Musk has sued Microsoft-backed company and Altman last year for straying from its founding mission. Following that, OpenAI counter-sued Musk earlier this year.
  • Aside from running xAI, Musk is also the head of Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), SpaceX and several other companies.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway acquires private rodent-control company
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) now lists Bell Laboratories, a rodent-control company based in Windsor, Wisconsin, as one of its operating subsidiaries.

Earlier in August, an industry publication and website, Pest Management Professional, reported that Bell Labs, a closely held company, had been purchased by Berkshire (NYSE:BRK.B) on July 31 and that it would continue as a Berkshire unit.

Financial terms of the deal weren't disclosed, according to the report.

data.title: Airbus U.K. strike postponed as workers prepare to vote on new pay offer
data.description:
Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) workers in Britain have postponed strikes planned for next month after the company presented an improved pay and pensions proposal, the Unite trade union said Friday.

Unite, which represents more than 3,000 aircraft fitters and engineers at Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY), had planned 10 days of strikes over demands for better pay, starting September 2-3 before more action on September 10-11 and another five days from September 15 depending on site-specific shifts.

Union members working at the company's Filton and Broughton factories will vote on the new offer in a ballot during September 12-19; if the offer is rejected, industrial action would resume on September 23-24, Unite said.

The union, whose members voted 90% in favor of taking industrial action, have said a strike would "disrupt production of wings for Airbus's core commercial and military aircraft programs."

data.title: Hearing on Lisa Cook's bid to stay on Fed board ends without a ruling
data.description:
A hearing on whether a federal judge should temporarily block President Donald Trump from removing Lisa D. Cook from the Federal Reserve's Board of Governors ended on Friday without a ruling, according to media coverage. The case will resume again on Tuesday.

The purpose of the hearing was to decide whether Cook can keep her job while her lawsuit, which seeks to permanently block Trump from firing her, proceeds. It's not considering the merits of the case or whether the president has the power to remove Cook from the central bank.

US District Judge Jia Cobb listened on Friday to arguments from Lisa Cook's attorney, Abbe David Lowell, and Department of Justice lawyer Yaakov Roth during the hearing that lasted about 90 minutes, Bloomberg News reported. Nothing more will happen with the case until Tuesday, when Cobb asked for a filing from Cook's lawyer and provided an opportunity for both sides to file additional arguments.

In Friday's hearing, Cobb expressed skepticism over on the Department of Justice's argument that the president has discretion in deciding what qualifies as "for cause," which can't be questioned by a court, Bloomberg reported. Roth said the government isn't arguing that he can remove officials at will.  

The judge commented that the logic behind the government's argument appeared to be "circular," because once a cause becomes part of a firing dispute, it allows the court to decide, at least broadly, what type of conduct qualifies as "cause."

Lowell also argued that the government did not give Cook time to defend herself via due process, according to the Bloomberg report. Within half an hour of FHFA Director William Pulte leveling mortgage fraud allegations against Cook via social media, Trump responded, also on social media, that she should resign. 

The judge also questioned Lowell on whether Cook suffered "irreparable harm" from the firing.

During the hearing, Cobb said she was "uncomfortable" with Lowell's argument that the president had acted out of an improper pretext. But she also had concerns with the government's arguments.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
